content.explanation:
🎉 You Built a Real Project. Now Let's Put It on the Internet.

Every lesson before this one happened inside this app's code editor — useful for learning, but invisible to the rest of the world. A project that only exists in a practice sandbox isn't a project yet; it's practice. This final lesson walks you through the exact same process every professional developer uses to take code from 'working on my screen' to 'live on the internet, with a real URL you can share.'

🧭 The Journey, In Plain Terms

By the end of this lesson, you will have done all four things you set out to learn:

  • 1. Built a working website — done, across Lessons 1-30
  • 2. Written code in a real code editor — done in this app's sandbox, and now you'll do it in a proper file
  • 3. Pushed code to GitHub — what this lesson teaches
  • 4. Deployed to a live URL via Netlify or Vercel — what this lesson finishes with

📂 What GitHub Actually Is

Think of GitHub like a save file for your project that lives online instead of just on your phone. Every time you 'commit' and 'push,' you're creating a saved snapshot of your code that's backed up safely, shareable with anyone, and — critically for this lesson — connectable to a deployment service that automatically builds and publishes your site whenever you update it.

🌍 What 'Deploying' Actually Means

Right now, your HTML file only exists on your device. A 'deployment service' like Netlify or Vercel takes your code from GitHub and hosts it on the internet, generating a real public URL like your-project.vercel.app — the same kind of link you've used to access this very course (cymorcodelearner09.vercel.app). Once deployed, ANYONE, anywhere, can visit that link and see your project — no code editor, no app, just a browser.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🧱 Step 1: Organize Your Project Files

Combine the HTML, CSS, and JavaScript skills from your checkpoints (Lessons 10, 20, 30) into real files, not just the in-app sandbox. A minimal project needs:

my-project/
  index.html
  style.css
  script.js

Link them together properly inside index.html:

<head>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
</head>
<body>

  <!-- your content here -->

  <script src="script.js"></script>
</body>

Notice <script> goes near the END of <body>, not in <head> — this ensures your HTML elements already exist on the page BEFORE your JavaScript tries to select them with querySelector, avoiding the exact mistake flagged back in Lesson 30's common mistakes.

🧱 Step 2: Create a GitHub Repository

  • 1. Go to github.com and sign in (create a free account if you don't have one)
  • 2. Tap the + icon → New repository
  • 3. Give it a name (e.g. my-first-website) — no spaces, use hyphens
  • 4. Set it to Public so it can be freely deployed
  • 5. Tap Create repository

🧱 Step 3: Push Your Code to GitHub (Phone-Friendly Method)

Since this course is built phone-first, the easiest method is GitHub's own web upload feature — no terminal or git commands required:

  • 1. On your new repository's page, tap Add file → Upload files
  • 2. Select your index.html, style.css, and script.js files
  • 3. Scroll down and tap Commit changes — this is GitHub's word for 'save this update'

If you later make code changes, you repeat this same process — GitHub will treat it as a new commit, keeping a full history of every version of your project.

🧱 Step 4: Deploy to Vercel or Netlify

Both work almost identically and both are free for personal projects like this:

Using Vercel:

  • 1. Go to vercel.com and sign in with your GitHub account
  • 2. Tap Add New → Project
  • 3. Select your repository from the list
  • 4. Leave the default settings (no special build needed for plain HTML/CSS/JS) and tap Deploy
  • 5. Within a minute, Vercel gives you a live URL like my-first-website.vercel.app

Using Netlify (alternative):

  • 1. Go to netlify.com and sign in with GitHub
  • 2. Tap Add new site → Import an existing project
  • 3. Choose your repository, leave default settings, tap Deploy site

From this point on, every time you push a new commit to GitHub, your live site automatically updates — this connection between GitHub and your deploy platform is exactly the same zero-budget workflow used to build and host real production tools, including the very app you're learning in right now.
content.common_mistakes:
⚠️ Common Capstone Mistakes

  • Forgetting to link your CSS/JS files correctly in index.html — your site will deploy successfully but look completely unstyled or non-interactive, because the browser can't find files that aren't properly linked.
  • Wrong file/folder capitalization — exactly like Lesson 4's image path warning, this becomes critical once deployed, since hosting servers are often case-sensitive even if your own device wasn't.
  • Setting the repository to Private on a free plan in a way that blocks the deploy platform from accessing it — keep it Public unless you specifically know how to configure private repo access.
  • Expecting instant updates — after pushing a new commit, give the deploy platform 30-60 seconds to rebuild before refreshing your live URL.

editor_sandbox.starter_code:
<!-- 🚀 This is a checklist lesson, not a code editor task. -->
<!-- Follow the steps above using your real GitHub and Vercel/Netlify accounts. -->
<!-- When you've completed all 4 steps, type the confirmation phrase below to finish the course. -->

capstone complete

mini_challenge.instruction:
🚀 FINAL TASK:

1. Combine your HTML, CSS, and JS into real files (index.html, style.css, script.js)
2. Create a GitHub repository and upload your files
3. Connect that repository to Vercel or Netlify and deploy it
4. Once your project has a real live URL, type this phrase into the editor to complete the course: capstone complete
mini_challenge.hint:
👉 If you're stuck:
- Make sure index.html links your CSS with <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css">
- Make sure your <script src="script.js"></script> is just before </body>
- On GitHub, use 'Add file → Upload files' for the easiest phone-friendly upload
- On Vercel or Netlify, sign in with GitHub so your repository shows up automatically

quiz_engine.question: What does connecting GitHub to Vercel or Netlify actually give you?
options: Nothing extra, it's purely for backup purposes
Your live site automatically updates every time you push a new commit to GitHub
It converts your HTML into a different programming language
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 Once connected, every push to GitHub triggers an automatic rebuild and redeploy — your live URL always reflects your latest committed code, with zero manual republishing needed.

takeaways: A project isn't finished until it's deployed somewhere real people can visit
GitHub stores and versions your code; deploy platforms publish it to a live URL
Always link your CSS and JS files correctly in index.html or they won't load once deployed
Once GitHub and your deploy platform are connected, every push auto-updates your live site
You've now completed the full journey: build → code → push → deploy, the same workflow real developers use every day

cheat_sheet.Project Structure: index.html, style.css, script.js in one folder
cheat_sheet.GitHub Upload: Add file → Upload files → Commit changes
cheat_sheet.Deploy (Vercel): vercel.com → Add New Project → select repo → Deploy
cheat_sheet.Deploy (Netlify): netlify.com → Add new site → Import existing project
cheat_sheet.Auto-Updates: Every push to GitHub triggers a new live deployment
